original_abstract: In the field of digital media steganography, the dominant paradigm involves embedding payloads by minimizing a carefully defined distortion function. Because modern coding techniques can already approach the theoretical payload-distortion bound, the central challenge for researchers is to design distortion functions that offer high resistance to empirical statistical detection. In this paper, we introduce Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD), a versatile framework for distortion design that is applicable across diverse embedding domains. This approach computes distortion as the sum of relative changes across coefficients in a directional filter bank decomposition of the cover image. By leveraging directionality, UNIWARD concentrates embedding modifications in regions that are inherently difficult to model, such as textures and noisy areas, while successfully avoiding smooth regions and clean edges. Experimental evaluations using both high-dimensional rich models and targeted attacks demonstrate that UNIWARD-based methods match or exceed the performance of current state-of-the-art schemes in the spatial, JPEG, and side-informed JPEG domains.
humanized_abstract: This paper discusses the new Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD) distortion metric for steganography. While current steganographic methods optimize some distortion metric in order to embed their payload as little as possible, it is today well known that most of the design choices for these metrics are driven more by algorithmic convenience than any theoretical grounds, given that nearly optimal performance is already demonstrated by practically all recent compression-based steganography. Thus, today’s major open question in the field is that of constructing good distortion metrics that are resistant to the types of empirical statistical detections that have come to replace threshold-based analyses. In this work, we focus on a metric which is designed to be versatile to as many possible embedding domains as possible, while having all the advantages one would wish for a distortion function (optimizing it for embedding will clearly be very good for detection resilience). We call this new metric — which works in the wavelet domain, as with all the current distortion metrics — Universal Wavelet Relative Distortion (UNIWARD). It works by summing the relative changes in coefficients within some (arbitrary) set of directional filter bank decompositions of the cover image. By making use of directional properties, the modifications induced by the embedding are then constrained to those areas in the image where they will be hardest to predict, that is, patterns, in particular, to textured regions, to salt & pepper noise, etc. These are precisely the locations where they will be hardest to statistically distinguish the data from randomness. On the other hand, it is easy to ensure that the modifications do not appear in flat, smooth regions, or in clean and defined edges, because the metric is clearly optimal (having no distortion) in these places. Our experiments that test steganographic capacity using high-dimensional, complex models as well as side-informed JPEG steganalysis also show that our new UNIWARD framework not only yields state-of-the-art performance for steganography in the spatial domain and in JPEG domain, but it also yields improvements in the side-informed JPEG steganalysis case, as compared with state-of-the-art steganographic algorithms in all three domains.
